serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kambing Goreng is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one or two pieces of fried mutton as served at a Mamak stall or hawker centre.

nutrient_source.title: Kambing Goreng — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kambing Goreng (cr_id F06060005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kambing Goreng. GI estimated at approximately 5 based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 1.1 g/100g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.07 per 120 g serving (GL = GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 5 × 1.32 / 100 ≈ 0.07). This is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 256 mg/100g — borderline; partially offset by estimated potassium ~380 mg/100g and dietary fibre 2.83 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g and request reduced salt in the marinade, as sodium at 256 mg/100g approaches the moderate-concern threshold; the estimated potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial cardiovascular offset but does not eliminate the need for portion control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.1 g/100g), zero sugar, high protein (34 g/100g) — negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kambing Goreng is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content (1.1 g/100g) and high protein (34 g/100g), which slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose stability; however, the fat content (14.68 g/100g) warrants portion moderation, and pairing with high-GI sides such as white rice should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 5.8 g/100g from red meat and coconut milk; frying preparation increases total fat and caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kambing Goreng in moderation (≤80 g per occasion) due to its saturated fat content (~5.8 g/100g) from both red meat and coconut milk, and should prefer pan-fried or grilled preparations over deep-frying to reduce total fat intake and limit AGE formation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (high arachidonic acid / omega-6), frying generates AGEs, saturated fat ≥5.8 g/100g — all amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kambing Goreng is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because the combination of red meat (high in arachidonic acid), deep-frying (generating advanced glycation end-products), and saturated fat (~5.8 g/100g) collectively amplifies joint inflammation; substituting with chicken or fish prepared with the same spice marinade is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kambing Goreng (炸羊肉 in Chinese; வறுத்த ஆட்டிறைச்சி in Tamil; तला हुआ मटन in Hindi) is a Mamak and Malay-style fried mutton dish popular in Singapore and Malaysia. The dish is prepared by first simmering mutton (goat or lamb) in spiced coconut milk — typically with turmeric, ginger, garlic, lemongrass, and chilli — before deep-frying or pan-frying until the exterior is golden and slightly crisp. The result is tender, flavourful meat with a rich, aromatic spice crust and a mildly creamy interior from the coconut milk marinade. Kambing Goreng is commonly served at Mamak stalls, hawker centres, and Malay restaurants as a main dish alongside rice, roti, or vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 273 kcal, 34 g protein, 14.68 g fat (approximately 5.8 g saturated), 1.1 g carbohydrate, zero sugar, 2.83 g dietary fibre, and 256 mg sodium. Its very low carbohydrate and zero sugar content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the saturated fat and sodium levels warrant moderation for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the red meat omega-6 burden and AGE generation from frying.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06060005) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb/mutton fatty acid profile as HPB does not disaggregate saturated fat; potassium estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb data. All other values are taken directly from the HPB source.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database; mutton prepared without added sugar and coconut milk contributes negligible sugar. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Kambing Goreng; USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb data consulted but not directly substituted to preserve source integrity. cholesterol_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g (~5.8 g/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (NDB 17290) as the closest approved-source analogue; documented as an adaptation. potassium_mg (~380 mg/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb data; used in hypertension health context assessment. dietary_tags in input included 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a mutton dish and has been removed. GI is estimated at 5 (analogous low-carbohydrate cooked meat) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries; GL per 120 g serving ≈ 0.07, clinically negligible.
